title: The Context Engineering Checklist
concept: A practical pass/fail checklist: budget respected, sources labeled, deduped, ordered, compressed where needed.

exercises.prompt: Quick recap: in the last lesson, you audited a context window. What were you hunting for?
exercises.explain: Auditing a context window means scanning for junk that shouldn't be there: repeated info, irrelevant filler, or important stuff stuck in the wrong spot. That audit skill sets you up for today's lesson: a checklist to catch those issues before you ship.

cards.title: Would You Ship This?
cards.body: Imagine handing in a school project without checking spelling, page order, or whether you copied the same paragraph twice. You wouldn't submit that. So why ship a context window without a checklist?

cards.title: The Context Engineering Checklist
cards.body: A simple pass/fail checklist you run before sending context to a model: is it within budget, are sources labeled, are duplicates removed, is it ordered well, and is it compressed where needed? If any box fails, you fix it before shipping.

cards.title: Like a Pilot's Pre-Flight Checklist
cards.body: Pilots don't rely on memory or gut feeling before takeoff, they run a checklist every single time: fuel, flaps, instruments. Context engineering works the same way. A quick checklist catches problems before they cause a costly crash mid-flight.

cards.title: Running the List
cards.body: You built a support-bot context: 3 docs, a chat history, and a user question. Checklist run: Budget? Under the token limit, pass. Labeled? Each doc says its source, pass. Deduped? One FAQ appeared twice, fail, remove it. Ordered? Most relevant doc was buried last, fail, move it up. Compressed? Long doc could be summarized, fail, shrink it.

cards.title: Myth: More Context Always Passes
cards.body: Stuffing in every possible document doesn't make a context window 'pass.' If it blows the budget, hides duplicates, or buries the answer, it fails the checklist even if it looks thorough. Passing means lean and well-organized, not maximal.

cards.title: Checklist Pass vs Fail
cards.body: Same five checks, two very different outcomes.

left.text: Over budget, unlabeled snippets, duplicate FAQ, random order, no compression on a huge doc

right.text: Within budget, every source tagged, duplicates removed, most relevant info first, long docs summarized

exercises.prompt: Which of these is NOT one of the five checklist items taught in this lesson?
exercises.explain: The checklist covers budget, labeling, dedup, order, and compression, all about the context window's content and shape. Model temperature is a generation setting, not part of preparing the context itself.

exercises.prompt: True or False: If a context window fits within the token budget, it automatically passes the full checklist.
exercises.explain: Fitting the budget is just one of five checks. The context could still fail because sources aren't labeled, duplicates exist, order is poor, or long text needs compression. All five checks matter.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank using a word from the checklist.
exercises.sentence: Before shipping context, make sure duplicate passages are removed, this is the deduped check.
exercises.explain: Deduped means removing repeated or duplicate information so the model doesn't waste space reading the same thing twice.

exercises.prompt: One of these context checklist summaries was AI-generated: vague, generically worded, and missing concrete specifics. Spot it.

options.text: Checklist passed: leveraging optimal contextual synergy, the payload has been holistically enhanced for maximum downstream efficacy and robust performance outcomes.
Checklist: 4/5 passed. Order needs fixing, the answer doc is currently third, move it to first since it directly answers the user's question.
Checklist failed on dedup: the refund policy paragraph appears in both doc 2 and doc 4, remove one copy to save 300 tokens.

exercises.explain: The first option is stuffed with vague buzzwords like 'synergy' and 'holistically enhanced' but says nothing concrete about which check passed or what to fix. Real checklist notes name specific docs, positions, and token counts, exactly like options b and c.

exercises.prompt: A teammate hands you a context window for review: it's within the token budget, and each source is tagged with its origin. But the same pricing table appears in two different docs, the least relevant doc is placed first, and a 2,000-word policy doc is included in full. What should you do?

options.text: Ship it, since budget and labeling already pass, that's good enough
Remove the duplicate pricing table, reorder so the most relevant doc comes first, and summarize the long policy doc before shipping
Just shrink the token budget further so everything gets cut automatically
Add more documents to give the model extra context to work with

exercises.explain: Passing 2 of 5 checks isn't enough, a real pass/fail checklist requires every item to check out: budget, labels, dedup, order, and compression. Here dedup, order, and compression all fail, so the fix is to remove the duplicate table, move the most relevant doc to the front, and compress the long policy doc into a summary. That's the discipline that separates a reliable context pipeline from a shaky one, and it's the core skill this capstone unit is building toward.
